name: Jewel Tide Flowing
description: The Jewel Tide Flowing, also known as Kanju or Manju, is one of the three sacred treasures of Japan, part of the Imperial Regalia. It is believed to be a magical jewel capable of controlling the tides, granting its wielder dominion over the sea. According to legend, the jewel was retrieved from the underwater palace of Ryūjin, the dragon god of the sea, by the trickster god Susanoo after he defeated the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi. The jewel's radiant light can summon and recede the ocean's waves at will, offering protection from invaders and control over maritime trade and travel. It symbolizes benevolence, prosperity, and the emperor's rightful rule, emphasizing their connection to the divine and the natural world. The Jewel Tide Flowing is not merely a beautiful object; it represents the power to nurture and sustain, reflecting the emperor's responsibility to safeguard the well-being of the nation. Its possession signifies the legitimate authority of the Imperial family and their ability to ensure the prosperity and safety of Japan through their connection to the kami and the natural forces of the world. The jewel's power is said to be linked to the spiritual purity of its holder, requiring a just and compassionate leader to wield its power effectively. Its loss would signify a catastrophic imbalance in the natural order and a crisis of leadership for the nation.
subtitle: Tidal control and Imperial power
origin: Retrieved from Ryūjin, the dragon god of the sea, by Susanoo.
currentLocation: Imperial Palace in Tokyo, Japan (believed to be)

keyMyths.title: Susanoo and the Serpent
keyMyths.description: Susanoo defeats Yamata no Orochi and discovers the Jewel Tide Flowing among the serpent's treasures, later presenting it to Amaterasu as a peace offering.

keyMyths.title: Empress Jingū's Conquest
keyMyths.description: Empress Jingū is said to have used the Jewel Tide Flowing to control the tides during her conquest of Korea, facilitating her naval invasion.

sources.author: Basil Hall Chamberlain
sources.description: Translation and commentary on the Kojiki, a primary source of Japanese mythology.
sources.text: Translation and commentary on the Kojiki, a primary source of Japanese mythology.

sources.author: William George Aston
sources.work: Nihongi: Chronicles of Japan from the Earliest Times to A.D. 697
sources.description: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another key source of Japanese mythology and history.
sources.text: Translation of the Nihon Shoki, another key source of Japanese mythology and history.

variants: Jewel of the Ebb and Flow

worshipPractices.name: Imperial Rituals
worshipPractices.description: The Jewel Tide Flowing, as part of the Imperial Regalia, is not directly worshipped but is integral to Imperial ceremonies. Its presence symbolizes the Emperor's authority and connection to the divine, particularly to the power and abundance of the sea. These rituals reinforce the Emperor's legitimacy and ensure prosperity for the nation.

worshipPractices.name: Shinto Purification Rites
worshipPractices.description: While not specific to the Jewel, water is often used in Shinto purification rites. Given the Jewel's association with the sea and tides, some may offer prayers for purification and safe passage on the water, implicitly acknowledging its power during these ceremonies. These rituals often take place near bodies of water.

festivals.name: Accession Ceremonies
festivals.timing: Upon the ascension of a new Emperor
festivals.description: The Jewel Tide Flowing is prominently displayed during the enthronement ceremonies. Its presence signifies the transfer of power and the Emperor's divine right to rule, reminding everyone of the continuity of the Imperial line and the blessings bestowed upon the nation.

festivals.name: Sea Festivals (Matsuri)
festivals.timing: Various times throughout the year, depending on the region
festivals.description: While not solely dedicated to the Jewel, sea festivals often feature prayers for safe voyages, abundant catches, and protection from storms. The Jewel Tide Flowing, as a symbol of control over the sea, may be invoked indirectly during these events.

cultural.modernLegacy: The Jewel Tide Flowing continues to be a potent symbol of Imperial authority and national identity in modern Japan. It frequently appears in art, literature, and popular culture as a representation of power, the sea, and the enduring legacy of Japanese mythology and the Imperial family. Its mystique and association with the divine ensure its continued relevance.

cross_cultural_parallels.similarity: Like the Jewel Tide Flowing, Brisingamen is a powerful and highly valued artifact, although its power relates to beauty and allure rather than control over the sea. Both are associated with divine figures and represent significant authority.

Poseidon's Trident grants him control over the seas, including the power to create storms and earthquakes, similar to the Jewel Tide Flowing's ability to manipulate tides. Both symbolize dominion over the marine environment.

Manannán mac Lir, a sea god, possesses various magical items, including a self-steering boat and a cloak of invisibility, reflecting his mastery over the sea. These items, like the Jewel Tide Flowing, symbolize power and control over the oceanic realm, though in a more diverse way.

Sekhmet's amulets, often imbued with protective magic, are powerful objects associated with a deity and designed to influence the world. The Jewel Tide Flowing, similarly, is an object of power linked to a divine purpose within its respective mythology.

associations.name: Water
associations.significance: The Jewel Tide Flowing is intrinsically linked to the ocean and its tides, symbolizing the power over water.

associations.name: Blue
associations.significance: Blue represents the vastness and depth of the ocean, reflecting the jewel's control over the seas.

associations.name: Moon
associations.significance: The moon's gravitational pull influences the tides, mirroring the jewel's power to manipulate them.

associations.name: Authority
associations.significance: As part of the Imperial Regalia, the jewel symbolizes the Emperor's authority and divine right to rule.

associations.name: Three
associations.significance: The Jewel is one of the Three Sacred Treasures, highlighting its importance within the Japanese Imperial lineage and spirituality.

associations.name: Prosperity
associations.significance: Control over the seas implies control over trade and resources, symbolizing prosperity and abundance.

associations.name: Dragon
associations.significance: Dragons are often associated with water and sea deities in Japanese mythology, signifying power and dominion over the ocean.

companions.name: Watatsumi
companions.description: The Dragon King of the Sea, Watatsumi, might be seen as an ally or guardian of the Jewel Tide Flowing, representing the power of the ocean it controls. He is a powerful sea deity that could be associated with protecting the jewel.

companions.name: Urashima Tarō
companions.description: Urashima Tarō, known for his journey to the Dragon Palace at the bottom of the sea, could be a figure entrusted with the jewel or one who benefitted from its power. He might be seen as a hero who at one point held or benefited from the jewel.

companions.name: Sea Turtles
companions.description: Sea turtles, often associated with Watatsumi and the sea realm, may act as messengers or guardians appointed to protect or serve the Jewel Tide Flowing. They are creatures of the sea and could be seen as protectors of the Jewel.

companions.name: Tide Spirits
companions.description: These elemental spirits embody the ebb and flow of the tides and would be intrinsically linked to the Jewel Tide Flowing, assisting its wielder. They embody the Jewel's power directly and act as helpers.

companions.name: Fishermen
companions.description: Those who live off the ocean would seek the Jewel Tide Flowing's blessing for safe passage and bountiful catches, making them potential allies. They depend on the sea and would seek the favor of the Jewel.

extendedContent.title: Creation and Forging in Myth
extendedContent.content:
The Jewel Tide Flowing's origins are deeply intertwined with the tumultuous relationship between Susanoo-no-Mikoto, the storm god, and his sister Amaterasu-ōmikami, the sun goddess. Following Susanoo's banishment from the heavenly realm after his disruptive behavior, he descended to Izumo, where he encountered an elderly couple grieving the impending sacrifice of their daughter, Kushinada-hime, to the eight-headed serpent Yamata no Orochi. Susanoo vowed to slay the serpent in exchange for Kushinada-hime's hand in marriage. After successfully defeating Yamata no Orochi, Susanoo discovered the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword within the serpent's tail. This sword, along with other treasures, was offered to Amaterasu as a sign of reconciliation.

The jewel itself is often associated with Ryūjin, the dragon god of the sea, and his underwater palace, Ryūgū-jō. While the Kojiki and Nihon Shoki don't explicitly detail the jewel's forging process, the narrative implies it was a pre-existing treasure within Ryūjin's domain. Later folklore, however, expands upon this, sometimes suggesting the jewel was crafted by the dragon god himself using solidified seawater and imbued with his immense power over the tides. This highlights the inherent connection between the jewel's power and the natural forces of the ocean, personified by Ryūjin. The acquisition of the jewel often involves a cunning scheme or act of bravery to either win it from Ryūjin or retrieve it after it's lost.

The tale of Urashima Tarō, a fisherman who visits Ryūgū-jō, provides further context. While not directly about the Jewel Tide Flowing, it illustrates the lavishness and magical nature of Ryūjin's palace, reinforcing the idea that it's a repository of powerful and extraordinary objects. The association with Ryūgū-jō elevates the Jewel Tide Flowing beyond a mere object; it becomes a symbol of divine authority and control over the marine realm, reflecting the deep respect and occasional fear the Japanese held for the unpredictable power of the sea.

extendedContent.title: Powers and Properties: Dominion Over the Sea
extendedContent.content:
The primary power attributed to the Jewel Tide Flowing is its ability to control the tides. The names 'Kanju' and 'Manju,' often translated as 'Tide-Ebbing Jewel' and 'Tide-Flowing Jewel' respectively, directly reflect this power. Legend states that by using the jewel, one could summon or recede the tides at will, granting immense control over the sea. This power is not merely a matter of influencing water levels; it signifies dominion over the marine environment and all its inhabitants.

Beyond tidal control, some accounts suggest the jewel possesses broader magical properties. These include the ability to calm storms, protect seafarers from harm, and even summon sea creatures. The jewel is often depicted as radiating a soft, ethereal light, suggesting an inherent connection to the spiritual realm and the divine power of the gods. This light is sometimes described as having a calming effect, capable of soothing troubled waters and dispelling negative energies.

The jewel's power is not unlimited, however. Its effectiveness likely depends on the wielder's spiritual purity and understanding of its inherent magic. Misuse or ill intentions could potentially backfire, leading to unpredictable and potentially disastrous consequences. This underscores the importance of respecting the natural world and wielding power responsibly, a recurring theme in Japanese mythology. The jewel's power is often depicted as a reflection of the user's own inner state, amplifying their intentions, whether benevolent or malevolent.

extendedContent.title: Notable Wielders: Emperors and Divine Figures
extendedContent.content:
The Jewel Tide Flowing, as part of the Imperial Regalia, is primarily associated with the Emperor of Japan, symbolizing his divine mandate and authority. While historical accounts rarely detail specific instances of emperors directly wielding the jewel to control the tides, its presence as a symbol of imperial power implies their inherent right to command the natural world. The Emperor's connection to the Jewel Tide Flowing reinforces his role as a mediator between the human and divine realms, responsible for ensuring the prosperity and safety of the nation.

Beyond the Emperor, Susanoo-no-Mikoto is also considered a significant figure connected to the jewel, albeit indirectly. While he is primarily known for retrieving the Kusanagi-no-Tsurugi sword, his victory over Yamata no Orochi and subsequent interaction with Ryūjin's realm establishes a link to the treasures of the sea. Although the original myths don't explicitly state Susanoo wielded the jewel, his association with the sea and his role as a trickster god capable of manipulating events suggest a potential for him to utilize its power.

Ryūjin, the dragon god of the sea, is arguably the most significant wielder of the jewel, albeit in a more fundamental sense. As the ruler of the oceans, he is the source of the jewel's power, and it represents his inherent dominion over the tides. While he may not actively 'wield' the jewel in the traditional sense, it serves as a symbol of his authority and the immense power he holds within his underwater kingdom. Ryūjin's association with the jewel reinforces the idea that true power resides in harmony with nature, rather than in attempts to control it.

extendedContent.title: Symbolism: Authority, Divine Mandate, and the Sea
extendedContent.content:
The Jewel Tide Flowing carries profound symbolic weight within Japanese culture. As one of the three Imperial Regalia, it represents the legitimacy and divine mandate of the Emperor. Its presence signifies the Emperor's connection to the gods and his responsibility to govern with wisdom and justice. The jewel's association with the sea further reinforces this notion, implying the Emperor's dominion over the land and its surrounding waters.

The jewel's ability to control the tides symbolizes the power to manage and regulate the natural world. In a nation heavily reliant on the sea for sustenance and trade, this control represents prosperity, security, and the ability to overcome adversity. The tides, in their ebb and flow, also symbolize the cyclical nature of life, death, and rebirth, reflecting the interconnectedness of all things.

Furthermore, the jewel represents the importance of respecting and honoring the power of the sea. The unpredictable nature of the ocean is a constant reminder of the forces beyond human control. The Jewel Tide Flowing serves as a reminder that while humanity can strive to understand and manage the natural world, it must always do so with humility and respect. The jewel, therefore, embodies the delicate balance between human ambition and the awesome power of nature, a central theme in Japanese mythology and cultural values.
